data.title: Fair Isaac: An Ace In The Hole Overlooked By The Market
data.description:
I rate Fair Isaac Corporation (FICO) a Buy. I think the market is completely missing the forest for the trees here. Investors are so spooked by the "triple threat" of regulation, new competitors, and AI disruption that they are ignoring a massive fundamental gap. This fear has sparked a brutal sell-off, dragging the stock down over 55% from its late-2024 peak to its current level near USD 1,070.

In this analysis, I want to show why these fears are mostly noise. FICO's management is already moving to neutralize these risks through an aggressive, high-margin strategic pivot. Most retail investors still think FICO is just a credit score company. It’s not. It is the universal language of global finance—a standard with switching costs that are frankly much higher than the current stock price suggests.

The urgency for investors is underscored by two major announcements this week. First, on April 16, 2026, FICO announced it will report its Q2 FY 2026 financial results on April 28, 2026. This upcoming call will be a critical pulse check on the company's aggressive SaaS transition. Second, the company recently priced USD 1.0 billion in Senior Notes, providing the firepower for its massive USD 1.5 billion share repurchase program. Management is essentially front-running the market, signaling that today’s price is a gift for long-term buyers.

With shares trading at roughly 24x forward earnings—levels we haven't seen in nearly a decade—the disconnect between FICO’s record Q1 FY2026 numbers and the share price is just too large to ignore. For a long-term investor, this is a rare entry point.

Let’s dive in.

Context

FICO’s Q1 FY 2026 results showed a healthy 16.3% top-line jump, but you have to look closer to see what's actually driving the bus. Most of the heavy lifting came from the Scores segment (both B2B and B2C), which surged nearly 30% year-over-year. Scores now make up about 60% of total revenue. On the flip side, the software side looks sluggish at first glance, posting just 1.57% growth.

Don’t let that software number fool you—it’s a total "optical drag." This tepid headline growth is actually the result of a deliberate, aggressive move to shift the business model. Under the hood, the numbers tell a different story: the high-priority platform (SaaS) business exploded by 37%, but that was masked by a 13% drop in the legacy, non-platform segment. This is exactly what a healthy transition looks like.

Operating earnings were up a solid 30%, though EPS growth trailed at just 7.6%. This gap isn't an operational failure; it's a math problem. FICO is dealing with higher interest expenses after issuing USD 1.5 billion in senior notes back in FY 2025. They traded in old debt for new obligations at the higher rates we’re seeing across the macro landscape.

Even with those interest rate headwinds, FICO managed to beat market expectations on both the top and bottom lines. More importantly, management isn't backing down—they’ve officially reiterated their FY 2026 guidance:

FICO is eyeing USD 2.35 billion in revenue for FY 2026, which would be a solid 18% jump over last year—a clear sign the business is actually picking up speed. While that forecast is already impressive, the real takeaway from the Q1 FY 2026 call was management's tone. They sounded remarkably confident about their ability to beat these numbers, suggesting this guidance is likely a conservative floor rather than a ceiling:

Steven Weber

Executive VP & CFO

Yes. Thanks, Manav. It's a good question. We're pretty confident we're going to be able to beat our guidance. I know we talked about it was pretty conservative last quarter. At this point, we're only 3 months in. There's just a lot of questions out in the macro environment. I mean, with the Fed today, it's just -- frankly, we don't probably know what numbers we would move to. So I think by next quarter, we'll have a much better idea of what the world looks like and what overall volumes are going to look like. So I think that was our thinking behind that.

Before jumping into the valuation, we need to look at how FICO actually makes its money—that’s where you’ll see why this volatility is such a gift for long-term buyers.

Business Model

FICO’s core mission is actually pretty straightforward: they use data to change how businesses make decisions. Today, the company operates as a high-margin, asset-light powerhouse that functions as the essential connective tissue between bank data and risk management.

They’ve essentially taken their legendary credit-scoring brand and hard-wired it into a massive cloud-native software suite. This allows the world’s biggest lenders and insurance providers to automate complex decisions in real time. We’re talking about a scale that is honestly difficult for competitors to replicate.

The Scores segment is still the heavy lifter, bringing in 60% of total revenue. It is the undisputed industry standard for credit in the U.S. This business works through two channels: B2B, where scores are baked into bank transactions every day, and B2C, where consumers pay for myFICO.com. At the end of the day, this segment is a financial tollbooth. It generates a massive stream of cash because it provides a "risk language" that the global credit market simply cannot function without.

Then you have the Software segment, which makes up the other 40%. This is the real growth engine. It isn’t just generic code; these are mission-critical tools for things like fraud detection and account origination. The centerpiece is the FICO Platform—a cloud-based (SaaS) setup that lets banks build their own custom workflows.

FICO is currently forcing a massive strategic pivot here. They are walking away from old-school, on-premise setups to move everyone onto this unified platform. It’s a shift from lumpy, one-off sales to high-retention, recurring revenue. Essentially, FICO is moving from a company that sells specialized tools to one that owns the entire decisioning ecosystem

The 10-K reality: a strong moat

I spent some time deconstructing FICO's recent 10-K filings to build out a few specific performance ratios. These metrics really get under the skin of the company's operations and show the true quality of the business:

Looking at the table above, the value creation strategy here is undeniable. We are seeing a climbing ROIC fueled by expanding FCF and gross margins, all supported by consistent double-digit revenue growth. Interestingly, R&D as a percentage of gross margin has actually trended down. This tells me FICO is scaling its profitability without having to dump disproportionate capital back into R&D—a clear sign of an efficient moat.

One thing that jumped out at me was the total debt-to-FCF ratio of roughly 4x. While that implies the debt could be cleared in four years—which isn't alarming—I usually expect a "moat" company of this caliber to sit closer to 1x. After a deep dive into the cash flow statements, the reason is clear: FICO is aggressively borrowing to fund share buybacks.

In FY 2025, they tapped the market for USD 1.5 billion in senior notes at 6%. On paper, the math looks tight. If you take the FY 2025 EPS against the stock price at that time, you get a yield of about 1.54%. It seems counterintuitive to borrow at a net cost of 4% (post-tax) to buy back stock yielding 1.54%. However, FICO’s diluted EPS grew nearly 30% last year. If they maintain even a fraction of that pace, the internal return will eventually dwarf the cost of debt. I’m generally not a fan of debt-funded buybacks at these valuations, but it shows management is incredibly bullish on their own double-digit EPS growth.

Finding a company that simultaneously grows ROIC, FCF margins, and gross margins for eight straight years is incredibly rare. Critics argue that FICO is just a score that can be swapped out. They’re wrong. FICO is a language. When a bank sells a loan, the investor knows exactly what a "720 FICO" represents.

Replacing it would require the entire financial ecosystem—from regulators to global investors—to learn a new dialect overnight. It won't happen. Furthermore, their software is "hard-wired" into bank IT systems. Swapping out FICO’s decisioning engine is like trying to perform a heart transplant on a marathon runner mid-race; it’s too risky and too expensive.

Even in a recession, FICO stays relevant. When the economy sour, lenders get nervous and rely even more on precise scoring to dodge defaults. It’s likely the last software a bank would ever turn off because it’s their main defense against credit losses.

Now that we've covered the core business, let’s look at why I'm so bullish—but first, I need to address the specific "market fears" that have been weighing on the stock.

Market Fear #1: Regulatory Challenges are likely Exaggerated

To gauge the real regulatory risk here, you have first to map out how the players in this ecosystem actually interact. You have the "Big Three" bureaus—Equifax (EFX), Experian (EXPGY), and TransUnion (TRU)—acting as the raw data engines. Then come the resellers, firms like MeridianLink or Factual Data, who "package" this data into reports for lenders. For everything except mortgages, a FICO score is standard; for mortgages, regulation currently mandates both FICO and the bureau-owned VantageScore.

Historically, FICO was somewhat buried inside the bureaus' servers. They provided the software, and the bureaus handled the pricing to the end-users. That changed in October 2025 with FICO’s Mortgage Direct License Program (MDLP). By shifting the workflow directly to resellers, FICO effectively cut out the middleman and reclaimed the power to set its own prices.

This move was a direct response to the FHFA’s push for a "bi-merge" (using two bureaus instead of three). Originally, the regulator wanted to cut costs by trimming a bureau out of the process, but that would have slashed FICO’s per-loan revenue by a third. Predictably, the bureaus panicked and pushed their own VantageScore as a cheaper alternative to protect their shrinking pie.

In the end, the FHFA backed down on the bi-merge but kept the "tri-merge" requirement while including VantageScore alongside FICO. The irony is classic: a policy designed to lower costs actually made mortgage reports more expensive because lenders now have to buy both scores. FICO seized the moment, hiked prices from a range of USD 0.60–USD 3.50 up to USD 10 per score, and moved to direct licensing to maintain total control.

Is a USD 10 fee on a USD 400,000 loan really "material"? I don’t think so, especially when that score is the primary defense against credit risk. While the market fears a regulatory crackdown on these price hikes, I think the FHFA has already created enough of a mess that they have little leverage left. Furthermore, FICO is using these hikes as a "carrot and stick" approach. They are charging FICO 10T prices for legacy scores, giving lenders and Government-Sponsored Enterprises (GSEs)—like Fannie Mae and Freddie Mac—a massive financial incentive to hurry up and migrate to the newer, more advanced 10T model.

That is incredible pricing power. But to be fair, we should look at the absolute worst-case regulatory scenario to see if the bear case holds any water.

Regulatory Worst-Case: How Much Damage Could They Really Do?

One nightmare scenario for the bears is the FHFA slapping a price cap on FICO and making the score optional while pushing VantageScore as the default. The goal would be to strip FICO of its pricing power and lower costs for consumers. But that plan hits a massive wall immediately: the secondary market.

The reality is that banks—from the "Too Big to Fail" giants to small community lenders—don't want to hold these loans for 30 years. They need the liquidity that comes from selling them to GSEs and global investors. And those buyers don't just want any data; they want FICO. Their decades of historical models are built on that specific language.

If regulators try to force VantageScore as the primary metric, the financial system would basically grind to a halt. Large banks can't just "flip a switch." They would have to prove to the OCC and the Fed that a new score predicts defaults just as accurately as the old one across every economic cycle. Most mid-size and community banks don't even have the quantitative teams to handle that kind of "Model Risk Management" headache. Under federal Third-Party Risk Management (TPRM) guidelines, the due diligence required to switch vendors is an operational mountain they aren't staffed to climb.

So, even if a regulator labels FICO "optional," the need for seamless loan saleability makes a transition a non-starter for most lenders. The odds are heavily in FICO’s favor here. Ironically, if the government tries to interfere, they’d likely just end up causing a mess for the very borrowers they're trying to help.

What about a hard price cap? If governing bodies decide FICO’s "toll-booth" is predatory and mandate a maximum fee, it would certainly sting. It would hit the highest-margin revenue stream and likely trigger a valuation de-rating. But again, this is unlikely. Global investors buying mortgage-backed securities expect the FICO standard. Forcing an unproven alternative could cause those investors to demand a risk premium, which would ironically drive mortgage rates higher.

Even in a worst-case "cap" scenario, FICO has a massive insurance policy: the FICO Platform. If they can’t charge per score, they will simply accelerate their move to enterprise SaaS subscriptions. By embedding their analytics directly into a bank's core IT infrastructure, they can recapture revenue through software fees. These enterprise contracts are far more complex and much harder for a regulator to "cap" than a standardized credit score.

Of course, a price cap wouldn't be painless. In the short term, it would definitely create a revenue air pocket since the entire financial system isn't yet fully migrated to the FICO Platform. However, FICO isn't a passive observer here; they have one of the most effective government relations arms in the industry. Even in an extreme scenario where a cap is signed into law, FICO’s ability to influence the implementation timeline is massive.

They can effectively slow down the "velocity" of these changes, dragging out the transition period for years. This gives the company the necessary runway to finish embedding its software into the bank's core systems, effectively moving the goalposts before the regulator can even blow the whistle.

Market Fear #2: Why the AI Disruption Threat is Mostly a Myth

The big bear case recently was that AI-native firms, like Upstart, would use machine learning to cherry-pick "better" borrowers than FICO could. The theory was that AI can digest millions of alternative data points—rent, utilities, even social media—to out-predict a standard three-digit score. But this narrative misses the fact that FICO has spent the last few years turning itself into a cloud-native SaaS powerhouse to neutralize this exact threat.

The real story in 2026 is the rapid takeover of the FICO Platform. This isn't just a scoring engine anymore; it’s an integrated cloud environment. As of early 2026, Platform ARR (Annual Recurring Revenue) is up 33% year-over-year, with a customer base of 150 and record software bookings hitting USD 102 million in 2025. Major players like MeridianLink and Xactus are jumping on board because this system lets them kill off their old, siloed decisioning tools and move everything into one scalable workflow.

The genius of the platform lies in its "Decision Intelligence." FICO has its own "Responsible AI" and Focused Sequence Models (FSMs), but they’ve kept the architecture open. This creates a clever "portal" effect. Instead of fintech startups trying to kill FICO, they’re actually fighting to get into the FICO Platform. They want to be an integrated service within a lender’s existing FICO pipeline because that’s the only way they can tap into FICO’s pre-built regulatory compliance and auditing tools.

Think about it from a bank’s perspective: a new AI startup can get through the front door much faster as a FICO "add-on" than it can by trying to rip out and replace the bank’s entire core engine. By making the platform the "operating system" for credit, FICO has essentially turned its potential disruptors into its best features.

Management has effectively turned the AI threat on its head. Instead of getting disrupted, FICO is positioning itself to be the tollbooth that every new AI model has to pass through.

Valuation

SA’s Quant ratings currently flag the stock as expensive with a "C" grade, largely because FICO is trading at a premium compared to its primary peers. However, to see if that premium is actually justified, I ran a valuation model using a set of conservative, "stress-tested" assumptions:

  • Share Count: 23.9 million (Q1 FY 2026).

  • FCF Margins: I’m modeling 30%. This is notably lower than the 5-year average of 34.28%, giving us a decent buffer if margins compress during the SaaS transition.

  • Time Horizon: A holding period through the end of 2030.

  • Revenue Growth: I’m sticking with management's 18% guidance for FY 2026, then tapering down to a steady 10% annually from 2027 through 2030.

  • Terminal P/FCF (2030): I’ve set this at 40x. While the multiple has swung wildly between 20x and 70x lately, it has consistently held above 40x for the last three years. Given the recurring nature of the platform revenue, 40x feels like a fair baseline for a double-digit grower.

  • Discount Rate: 9.5%. I usually look for 7-9% for a "moat" business like this, but I’ve hiked it to 9.5% here. This builds in an extra margin of safety to account for the ongoing noise around AI and regulators.

Based on these inputs, here is how the math shakes out:

Using these inputs, I’ve mapped out FICO’s revenue path through 2030. I stayed on the conservative side here: I took management’s 18% guidance for FY 2026 and then dialed it back to a steady 12% annual growth from 2027 through 2030. That brings us to a projected top line of USD 3,696 million by the end of the decade. Applying a 34% FCF margin to that figure gives us an estimated free cash flow of USD 1,257 million for 2030.

When you divide that cash flow by the 23.9 million shares currently outstanding, you get an FCF per share of 52.5. At a 40x multiple, we’re looking at a 2030 target price of USD 2,098 per share.

To find what that’s worth today, I discounted it back to 2026 using my 9.5% hurdle rate:

Intrinsic value = 2098/(1+discounted rate)^5.

Crunching those numbers leaves us with an approximate intrinsic value of USD 1,332.9 per share. At the current price of USD 1,069, that translates to a 24.6% margin of safety. In my view, FICO has a clear path to beat my 12% annual revenue growth assumption, especially since the broader analyst consensus is significantly more bullish on their trajectory through 2030.

Given the current "SaaS-apocalypse" sentiment and the headlines around new regulations, the stock is going to be bumpy. If you’ve decided this is a business you want to own for the long haul, the best approach is a slow, methodical accumulation. I wouldn't rush in with a full position right away. Building your stake gradually over the coming months will allow you to capitalize on the price swings we’re likely to see, which could very well offer even more attractive entry points than what we see today.

That said, even a high-conviction play needs to be stress-tested. Before you commit significant capital to a position, you should carefully weigh these specific risks.

Risks

The most immediate threat to this thesis is the sheer complexity of the cloud migration itself. This "heart transplant" of bank IT systems is inherently messy. If the integration hits a wall, the resulting friction could leave FICO exposed to AI-native disruptors while stalling FCF growth. However, Q1 2026 results suggest we’ve hit a tipping point. With Platform ARR accelerating 33% and ACV bookings hitting record highs, the market is signaling that the cost of staying on legacy infrastructure is now higher than the pain of moving to the cloud.

That said, investors must watch the "Transition Gap." FICO’s 122% Platform NRR—meaning existing clients are spending 22% more each year—is impressive, but the 91% NRR in the legacy business shows a deliberate cannibalization. The risk is twofold: if the old business erodes faster than the new platform can scale, or if platform adoption plateaus after this initial wave, FICO will struggle to justify its premium multiple. While I believe management has their hands on the levers here, a miscalculation in the migration's velocity would lead to a significant re-rating.

There is also the "multiple risk." My valuation assumes a 40x P/FCF, which is a haircut from the 50x and 78x multiples we saw in 2025 and 2024. However, if the regulatory noise doesn't fade, or if the "monopoly" narrative gains actual teeth, that 40x multiple could still be too generous. While FICO’s government relations team has successfully protected the "toll-booth" in the past, a shift in political sentiment is a variable even the best lobby can't always control.

Finally, we have to talk about the aggressive balance sheet. FICO is effectively running a "negative spread" by issuing debt at 6% to buy back stock with a 1.5% earnings yield. This is a high-conviction bet that future growth will dwarf the cost of capital. If a recession hits or the software transition stumbles, that $2.5 billion debt load becomes a heavy anchor.

Fortunately, management has been smart about the "maturity wall," pushing principal payments out to 2033 and 2034. This gives the SaaS platform nearly a decade to scale before the bill comes due. Combined with an asset-light model and the ability to offset interest costs with surgical price hikes, FICO has built a sturdy buffer. If you can stomach these specific risks and believe the mitigation strategies hold water, it becomes a question of how much room this "operating system of credit" deserves in your portfolio.

Takeaways

FICO remains a compelling play because the market's biggest fears—AI disruption and regulatory crackdowns—appear to be well-managed by the company’s pivot to a platform-centric model. While it’s possible that regulators could force a short-term price cap, the actual cost of a FICO score is such a rounding error in a $400,000 mortgage that lenders are unlikely to revolt. They aren't paying for a number; they are paying for a defensive shield against credit losses.

Even if the FHFA moves forward with a price cap, FICO has already built its "exit ramp." By accelerating the migration to its cloud-based platform, management can pivot away from per-score pricing toward enterprise software fees. This effectively "future-proofs" the revenue stream, moving it into an area where regulators have far less leverage and where AI becomes a feature of the platform rather than a threat to it.

Ultimately, the noise in the headlines creates an opportunity for the rational investor. While the "SaaS-apocalypse" and regulatory narratives dominate the news cycle, the structural moat here is actually widening. FICO isn't just surviving the transition to modern finance; it’s building the operating system for it. For those willing to look past the immediate volatility, the long-term compounding potential remains very much intact.

data.title: KWEB: Earnings And The AI Trade In China ETFs
data.description:
AI has been a growing contributor to revenue for China's internet companies. The KraneShares CSI China Internet ETF (KWEB) is a China AI ETF that provides exposure to the major players in China's internet and AI ecosystem.

The companies in KWEB increased their total revenue attributable to cloud computing and AI services by 13% year-over-year in the fourth quarter of 2025.1 At current levels, we estimate that the annual revenue earned from cloud computing and AI services in the China AI ETF could reach over $50 billion in 2026.1

Below, we summarize the key developments in each of the subsectors within KWEB in the fourth quarter of 2025.

AI & Cloud

Revenues increased 25%. Tencent (TCEHY) made WeChat and QQ compatible with the popular international agentic AI platform Open Claw. Meanwhile, Alibaba (BABA) released the latest version of its Qwen large language model (LLM). Alibaba also projected cloud revenues doubling every year over the next ﬁve years. And its chipmaking unit, T-Head, is on track to reach RMB 10 billion in annual revenue. That would bring T-Head close to the annual revenue of Cambricon, a leading China-based fabless chipmaker. OpenAI (OPENAI) announced it would be closing its Sora video creation platform, effectively ceding the space to Kuaishou’s (KUASF) Kling AI globally. Kling AI had already achieved monthly active users (MAUs) of nearly 12 million, while Sora had only reached 2 to 4 million MAUs.

Local Services

Local Services revenues for KWEB companies increased 78% in the fourth quarter. Strong consumer subsidy spending from JD (JD), Meituan (MPNGF), and Alibaba ensured consistent user retention and steady volume increases while weighing on proﬁtability. JD, in particular, tripled revenue from JD Now, a specialized service offering delivery of various consumer goods within 30 minutes to urban consumers. However, growth could slow as the platforms have agreed to reduce spending on promotions and prioritize proﬁtability.

Education

Education platform TAL (TAL) increased revenue by 27%. TAL reported strong growth in its online and its ofﬂine tutoring programs. Also, its Singles’ Day promotion season was successful. TAL also unveiled a new learning device for children ages six to eleven at the 2026 Consumer Electronics Show (CES) in Las Vegas, where it received a “CES Picks” award.

Healthcare

Healthcare platforms JD Health and Ali Health reported results for the second half of 2025. Their revenue increased 18%, on average. Ali Health beat estimates on revenue and net income for the full year of 2025. Both platforms beneﬁted from a shift in strategy by many pharmaceutical companies from hospital procurement to direct online sales. This shift has been partly driven by policy reforms in recent years.

Recruitment

Kanzhun (BZ), which operates the popular “BOSS Zhipin” job search app, increased revenue 14% in the fourth quarter. Management noted strong demand for employment driven by Lunar New Year holiday consumption trends. Meanwhile, blue-collar jobs outperformed placements for white-collar roles.

Entertainment

Entertainment revenues increased 13%. Bilibili (BILI) and Tencent Music beneﬁted from improved monetization for their premium subscribers.

Logistics

Logistics revenues increased 11%. JD Logistics (JDLGF), a logistics spinoff from JD.com, beneﬁted from increased demand from its parent platform. Meanwhile, Full Truck Alliance (YMM), sometimes referred to as the “Uber of trucking”, increased revenue and proﬁtability in the fourth quarter. Full Truck benefited from efforts to overhaul its model to favor commission-based orders rather than wholesale future capacity sales. The company also reported a 12% increase in completed orders.

Social Media

Social media revenues increased 10% on average, led by Tencent’s WeChat, which beneﬁted from a pickup in advertising spending driven by new AI tools. Meanwhile, despite seeing more tepid growth than WeChat, Weibo noted strong growth in the e-commerce and auto segments of its social media advertising sales and expects this momentum to continue throughout 2026.

Travel

Travel revenues increased 9%. High demand for domestic travel among China’s consumers, especially for experience-based bookings, led to decent growth for travel booking platforms. Trip.com (TCOM) saw strong growth in both its overseas business and domestic China travel. Gaming revenues increased 6%. However, key game makers within KWEB delivered highly variable results. Tencent, the growth leader for the quarter, saw international games growth outpace domestic games growth by a signiﬁcant margin, on the back of popular international titles like PUBG. Meanwhile, NetEase’s (NTES) revenue was lower than expected in the fourth quarter as demand for legacy titles was subdued. Finally, XD turned proﬁtable thanks to improving economics for its game-sharing platform, known as TapTap, in China.

E-commerce

E-commerce revenues increased a modest 4%, challenged by a high base in the ﬁnal quarter of 2025. Trade-in subsidy usage has declined since then, as many households have already taken advantage of the program by upgrading home appliances and other large items. Meanwhile, core domestic commerce growth was sluggish for both JD.com and Alibaba, though PDD Holdings (PDD) experienced strong growth in both domestic and international commerce thanks to an advertising push and expansion into lower-tier cities and rural areas.

FinTech

FinTech revenues also increased 4%, led by insurance platform Zhong An, which beneﬁted from growth in new categories such as pet and drone insurance. Meanwhile, growth was challenged for consumer lending platforms like Qﬁn, which faced regulatory caps on interest rates and new loan origination rules that tightened liquidity. Meanwhile, Tencent’s WeChat Pay continued to experience strong growth.

Live Streaming

Live streaming revenues increased 4%. East Buy (KLTHF) rebounded from the loss of some celebrity key opinion leaders (KOLs) in 2024. Meanwhile, Kuaishou (KUASF) experienced a slight decrease in its live streaming revenue, but reported upgrades to its live streaming platform, such as the introduction of multi-host live streaming events as well as the integration of live-streamed products into AI suggestion models.

Traditional Search

Traditional search revenue for Baidu (BIDU) has hit record lows, down 15%, as AI-powered search continues to take market share. This led the company’s CEO to effectively declare that AI has become the “new core” of Baidu during the company’s earnings call.

Autos

Auto platform Autohome (ATHM) reported an -18% decrease in revenue, driven by lower advertising spending from internal combustion engine (ICE) automakers. Meanwhile, the platform faced strong competition from short-video platforms for advertising spending from electric vehicle (EV) brands. Lead generation and customer support services for dealers were more resilient but did not offset declines in the company’s core businesses.

Real Estate

Real estate platform KE Holdings (BEKE) reported a 29% decline in revenue, which its management attributed to a high base effect for the new home transactions in the ﬁnal quarter of 2025. Overall, price recovery in China’s residential real estate market remains gradual.

Citations:

  1. Data and estimate from Bloomberg and KraneShares as of 3/31/2026.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Markets Weekly Outlook: A Real Peace Process Or A Fantasy?
data.description:
By Elior Manier

Week in review: A proper peace process unfolding; will it lead to an actual deal?

This week has been nothing short of historic. Both the Nasdaq and S&P 500 have charged to fresh all-time highs, completely leaving the geopolitical panic behind as traders aggressively price in a proper peace agreement. The move has bulldozed through any type of resistance and prior records in a move that has left many traders scratching their head.

Only the Dow Jones is looking to catch up to its younger peers but is already on pace to do so – that is, if the current pricing withstands the weekend.

While the past two weeks of US-Iran negotiations have generated their fair share of chaotic headlines, the diplomatic process unfolding in Pakistan appears genuinely serious, with both sides making significant, market-moving concessions.

The absolute catalyst for the week was this morning's market-rocking news regarding the reopening of the Strait of Hormuz. Bolstered by President Trump's remarks that he expects a finalized deal in a day or two, the geopolitical risk premium imploded.

Oil prices collapsed nearly 10% since yesterday, completely erasing their previous rally to trade comfortably right below the $90 handle. Notably, clear signs of insider trading emerged in the crude market just before the announcement - a continuation of the wild market craziness that has defined Trump’s second term, but certainly not a first.

Moving forward, physical traders will be closely monitoring the strait to see if actual tanker flows resume. The euphoria isn't limited to traditional equities paying out big peace dividends.

Cryptocurrencies caught a massive bid, with Bitcoin (BTC-USD) exploding back to life and rallying to sit just below the $80,000 (~$78,000) mark as the weekend approaches, also boosting other crypto assets.

On the macroeconomic front, the reality of the recent commodity shock is setting in. Both US CPI and PPI inflation reports rose strongly, although optimists will console themselves as they missed their most extreme upside expectations.

However, this energy-driven jump could merely be the beginning of a much more significant inflationary wave hitting the economy over the coming months.

This week will provide fresh insights on inflation in other countries, including Japan, Canada and the UK. Now, participants are bracing for a pivotal week.

The current ceasefire officially expires on April 22. Without a formal extension or a signed peace deal, this historic progress could vanish in a flash, throwing markets back into extreme volatility. An actual deal will be mandatory to sustain the rally.

Weekly Performance across Asset Classes

As you can see, when oil suffers, everybody dances. Even with the commodity gapping higher at the beginning of the week, stock markets have continued to explode higher, and shortly after, everything followed.

WTI Crude is down 10% since the beginning of the week and off 17% from its weekly opening gap. The most risky assets have naturally outperformed the recovery, with cryptos (ETH-USD and altcoins) on top, silver dominating the metals market, and Nasdaq dominating global stock benchmarks.

The Week Ahead: Major Inflation data coming up for Canada, the UK and Japan

Traders will have to get ready for a roller-coaster week, with macroeconomic data and major peace headlines on schedule.

Asia Pacific Markets – Japanese Inflation

Japan is under heavy pressure regarding their future monetary policy, and with the inflation report for March incoming, where the first effects of energy price hikes will be felt, the moment could be decisive.

The release is expected on Thursday evening (7:30 P.M.). A large beat could confirm a rate hike at the end of the May meeting if economic conditions don't worsen by then.

Bank of Japan representatives did refuse to comment on the issue during the IMF Meeting.

Europe and UK Markets – A Focus on the UK and Germany data

GBP traders will have a lot on their plate in the coming week, with a three-streak combo:

UK employment, inflation, and retail sales provide insights on the state of the economy and price rises as participants prepare for an economic shock.

Euro traders will have to pay close attention to the ZEW Economic Sentiment Survey and German PMIs that could also reshape forward-looking pricing for the Old Continent.

North American Markets – Rare releases in the US, Geopolitics, and Canadian Inflation

The US takes a relative break from economic data, only releasing retail sales on Tuesday, and leaves space for continued price discovery. Keep in mind that past week movements will be contingent on a sustainable peace deal with Iran, with the talks expected throughout the weekend.

CAD traders will also have to reprice chances of future hikes with Canadian inflation opening the North American week on Monday. A 2.5% consensus is announced, but energy price rises could definitely point to a beat on such low expectations.

Next Week's High-Tier Economic Events

Daily Market Wrap

The Hormuz reopening news was icing on a very bullish cake to conclude this extremely positive week. The heaviest beta assets did what they did best and kept exploding higher across the asset map - cryptos and silver finished on top, both up around 3% on average.

US benchmarks kept extending further to their newfound peaks, with the Dow Jones catching up and concluding the session on top. On the other side, crude oil took a 10% beating after the news but somewhat bounced as the session went by – expect a lot of movement in the commodity in the coming week.

Safe trades and an enjoyable weekend!
